tactic_preview: $23K MRR SaaS. 25 hours/week. Refuses to scale. Contentment angle for build-in-public community.
page_title: Why I stopped trying to scale my $23K MRR SaaS
draft_hook:
my saas makes $23k mrr.

i work 25 hours a week.

everyone tells me i should scale.

i don't want to.

here's why that's the right decision for me (and maybe for you):

revenue per hour worked: $920.

if i hire a team, onboard, manage, review — i get to $80K MRR but work 60h/week for $1.3K/hour.

that's a 40% raise for 140% more hours.

no thanks.

the scaling obsession is a twitter metric for people who want to look big.

$23K MRR working 25h/week is a life.
thread_hook:
25 hours/week. $23K MRR.

my investors tell me to scale.

my twitter feed tells me to hire.

here's the math that changed my mind:

1/ my current revenue per hour worked is $920
2/ scaling to $80K MRR requires a team, which caps me at $1.3K/hour but adds 35 hours of management
3/ net result: 40% raise for 140% more work

the 'scale or die' mentality is VC math projected onto solo founders.

it doesn't apply if you own 100% of a profitable thing.

know your number. defend it. ignore the rest.

tactic_preview: What separates $0 Etsy digital products from $500/mo ones. Directly applicable to our 22 PDFs.
page_title: What $500/mo Etsy digital products have in common (and what $0 ones do wrong)
draft_hook:
the difference between an etsy digital product that makes $0 and one that makes $500/month:

it isn't quality. it isn't price.

it's discoverability + specificity.

$0 product: 'productivity planner'
$500/mo product: 'notion planner for ADHD freelancers — weekly template with time blocking'

same product. completely different title.

one shows up for 'productivity planner' (100K competition).
one shows up for 'notion ADHD freelancer template' (12 competition, buyer intent keyword).

specific wins. always.
thread_hook:
i've watched 200 etsy digital product shops.

the ones making $500+/mo all have these 4 things:

1/ ultra-specific title (niche + format + use case)
2/ 7-12 product images showing the product IN USE
3/ instant download framing ('download immediately')
4/ 1 review (social proof breaks the zero-review cold start)

the $0 shops:

1/ generic titles
2/ one mockup image on white background
3/ no 'what you get' clarity
4/ waiting for organic to kick in

specificity is the distribution channel. not SEO.

tactic_preview: Electricians on GoDaddy miss the spring renovation surge. 'Panel upgrade near me' queries spike March-May.
page_title: Why electricians lose spring renovation clients (and how to fix it in 48 hours)
draft_hook:
found scotchman electric out of garrettsville, ohio.

in business 57 years. licensed LLC. BBB accredited.

website: godaddysites.com subdomain.

march is when homeowners start booking electrical work — panel upgrades, ev charger installs, whole-home rewires.

'electrician near me' in garrettsville right now returns everyone except scotchman electric.

57 years in business, invisible during the exact season that drives 40% of annual revenue.

we pitched. 2-day build. custom domain. local SEO. map listing.

that's all it takes.
thread_hook:
3 electricians I found this week:

57 years in business — GoDaddy subdomain.
54 years in business — GoDaddy subdomain.
30 years in business — Wix free plan.

none of them rank in Google for 'electrician [city]'.

all three are in their busiest season.

here's why this keeps happening:

1/ the default website the electrician's nephew set up in 2012 is still running
2/ GoDaddy free subdomains have ZERO local SEO authority
3/ 'electrician near me' is dominated by whoever has a $15 domain and a Google Business listing

the fix costs less than a service call.

the cost of not fixing it is the entire spring renovation season.

tactic_preview: Veteran-owned local businesses carry a trust signal that converts — but it's buried or missing from their online presence.
page_title: The veteran-owned trust signal that converts — and why most vet businesses don't use it
draft_hook:
hensley electric in galveston, texas.

veteran-owned.

facebook DM contact. no real website.

'veteran-owned' is one of the highest-converting trust signals for local services.

homeowners actively filter for it.

but if you're running on facebook with no website, the search never reaches you.

you're a veteran who built a business. the people who want to support that can't find you.

we fix that.
thread_hook:
veteran-owned local businesses have a massive untapped trust signal.

homeowners search 'veteran-owned electrician near me'.
'veteran-owned plumber'.
'veteran-owned HVAC'.

conversion rates on veteran-owned searches run 15-25% higher than generic.

but 80% of veteran-owned local service businesses have no online presence that surfaces that signal.

facebook page. no website. GoDaddy subdomain with no mention of their service history.

the fix: custom domain + 'veteran-owned and operated since [year]' above the fold + Google Business verification.

3 steps. 48 hours. they start showing up for a search term with zero competition and premium conversion rates.

tactic_preview: Tax prep businesses invisible during their 6 most important weeks. April 15 = hard deadline. GoDaddy free subdomain = no Google ranking.
draft_hook:
there are tax prep businesses in the US running on GoDaddy free subdomains.

april 15 is in 27 days.

they're invisible in every local google search during the 6 weeks that drive 60-70% of their annual revenue.

not because they're bad at taxes.

because nobody told them their website is costing them clients.

we did.
thread_hook:
found 4 tax prep businesses this week with one thing in common:

godaddy free subdomain. april 15 in 27 days.

here's why that combination is a business killer:

1/ GoDaddy free subdomain = google treats it as temporary. no domain authority, no local SEO, no map ranking.

2/ 'tax prep near me' searches spike 400% in march-april. the businesses invisible in this window miss their biggest revenue window.

3/ these aren't struggling businesses. they've been operating for 10-20 years. they're just running on the default website the accountant set up in 2014.

the fix is a $50 domain + 2 hours of setup. the cost of not fixing it is the entire march-april search window.

this is the pitch that writes itself.

tactic_preview: Spring = plumbing/HVAC/real estate demand spike. Businesses on Google .business.site miss it entirely.
draft_hook:
spring is when plumbers, HVAC techs, and realtors get busy.

also when it matters most that people can find them in google.

found a plumber in la mesa with google .business.site as his main website.

if you google 'plumber la mesa', he doesn't exist.

he's been in business 8 years.

he just doesn't have a real website.

tactic_preview: Habit Pixel: $0 to $1K MRR in 8 months, solo dev, no funding. We have 114 apps.
draft_hook:
habit pixel went from $0 to $1K MRR in 8 months.

no funding. no team. solo dev.

differentiated on aesthetics (pixel art canvas), not features.

we have 114 apps. each one a different niche, a different angle.

their 8 months proves it's possible. our 114 bets multiply the odds.
thread_hook:
habit pixel hit $1K MRR with one niche habit tracker.

here's what they did that most indie devs skip:

1/ niche beats generic every time. 'pixel art habit tracker' is not competing with Streaks or Habitica. it's a different customer.

2/ aesthetics = retention. people screenshot their pixel canvas. shareability built-in.

3/ solo, bootstrapped, no paid acquisition. just App Store + indie hacker posting.

we have 114 apps across habits, faith, fitness, meditation, journal, ADHD.

each one targeting a specific niche where the generic player is weak.

the math: if Habit Pixel hits $1K MRR from one niche, 114 niches = the portfolio wins.

tactic_preview: FaithTime owns generic faith search. We own denomination-specific search. Zero competition.
draft_hook:
faithtime.ai is publishing 'best faith apps 2026' comparison articles.

they own the generic search intent.

we have 13 denomination-specific apps deployed: Catholic, Baptist, Lutheran, Methodist, Presbyterian, Episcopal, Pentecostal, Evangelical, Anglican, Orthodox, Protestant, Shia, Sunni.

FaithTime can't write 'best Baptist habit tracker 2026' without admitting they're not Baptist-specific.

we can. and nobody else has.
action_detail: One per denomination: 'best [denomination] habit tracker 2026'. FaithTime owns generic; we own specific. Zero SEO competition for denomination queries.

tactic_preview: Creed 199K users (not 1.2M). Denomination-specific angle more credible, not less.
draft_hook:
the competitor we've been benchmarking against?

creed: bible chat.

yesterday's report said 1.2M users. fresh research says 199K.

the 1.2M was their own marketing copy.

actual: 199K users, 7,360 reviews, 4.89 stars.

revised pitch: 'Creed has 200K users with a generic approach. we're building denomination-specific versions.'

200K is still proof the market exists. it's just 6x more capturable than we thought.

tactic_preview: Opal raised to $239/yr. FocusLock is free. 23x price gap = positioning write-up.
draft_hook:
opal: screen time control.

price: $239/yr.

rating: 4.79 stars.

we built focuslock-web. free.

the opal user who just got hit with a $239 renewal email is now searching for alternatives.

we should be the answer they find.
action_detail: Write 'Opal alternative — free screen time app' comparison page. Target: 'opal screen time alternative 2026'.

tactic_preview: Lemlist raised $10/user in Feb 2026. Every Lemlist user is a warm EAS prospect.
draft_hook:
lemlist raised prices in february 2026.

$79/user/month. was $69.

3-person team: $237/mo minimum. plus inbox add-ons.

managed cold outreach at $500-2,000/month suddenly looks different when your DIY stack costs $237 before labor.

if you're paying $79/user for lemlist in 2026, there's a better calculation.
